sentences.text: They-- for-- somehow, they knew there were six people there.
And they captured five.
So they were looking.
And they spotted me running.
So they actually came to the opening of the-- of that drainpipe.
But the drainpipe went across the road, underneath the road.
And it wasn't straight.
So when they looked in, they shined in a flashlight, it was on-- an angle off.
And I was sitting like in the middle.
And they didn't spot me.
This is October-- yeah, it's October, '42.
So-- and then I heard when they took those-- my cousins away, they walked right on top of me, when-- And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sat there for a while.
I really didn't know what to do.
I was afraid to go out because I figured one of them must be sitting there and waiting for me somewhere because they knew that I'm in the area.
And-- But then I heard the roosters crow.
So I was getting worried because usually, like, they do at twilight, you know, early morning.
And I had to get out before it got light.
Otherwise, I would be in trouble.
So I slowly stuck my head out.
And it was quiet.
Nobody was there.
And I came out.
And I'm walking sort of in the direction of Volodymyr.
And, and then I came to a crossroads.
And I really didn't know where to go.
And I was very tired and sleepy.
And I saw a farm on the right side with some stacks of hay.
And so I decided to-- to pull in, get into one of those stacks.
And I fell asleep.
In the morning, the, the woman from the household got up to feed the livestock, the cows.
And she noticed that the stack was disturbed.
So she thought some animal was in there.
So she ran back in, took a pitchfork.
And she comes running.
And I-- I spotted it.
And I put my hand out, and say, no, no, it's me.
So she got scared.
She ran and called her husband.
Her husband came out.
And he says, who are you.
And being that I wasn't too far from Lyuboml', I said-- and my uncle was known.
So I said that Falek Ginsburg is my uncle.
So he went back and took a few bundles of straw and put them around the hole where I was.
And he said, I'll let you stay there till the evening.
And then you have to leave.
And he wanted to know what happened.
And I explained that we got captured.
And then he was going to leave.
So he came to me and said, we are going away.
It was on a Sunday.
Because they were going to church, to that same town where we got captured.
So he says, I'll find out what happened.
He went there .
And he came back.
He said, the two-- the two boys they shot right away in the woods.
And the women, they kept.
They probably-- you know, they probably, the way the normal thing.
They probably raped them, abused them, eventually shot them because nobody survived.
Then in talking to him, I asked him about that Scleba that used to deal with my mother.
And I knew the village, the village where they lived was [INAUDIBLE].
And I asked him.
He says, yeah, that's about five miles from here.
So I made my way there.
And that was a-- that was a good lead because otherwise, I don't know where I would have gone and what.
So-- But he was afraid to keep me because he had some connections with Jews he was helping.
And the people, they were suspicious of him.
So I-- what I used to was hang around in the woods.
And then at night, sneak into his barn and sleep there.
And then early in the morning, get out again.
But it was getting cold.
So he told me that he's traveling to Volodymyr.
He has to do some business there, buying things, maybe bringing food to the Jews in the ghetto.
So I said, I'll go there.
Maybe I can get some food, some clothes.
So I went with him.
And I got into the ghetto.
I did manage to get-- to find a sheepskin that was cut up.
People, probably somebody cut it open.
They were looking for money or something.
And it was big.
And I found some shoes that were both, both left foot.
And so I took that.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [DRINKS] He was supposed to meet me a week later on a Tuesday morning.
And I managed to get out.
The ghetto was already mostly liquidated.
There were only like 2,500 people left there out of a population of 20,000 roughly.
And there was barbed wire, you know.
And you couldn't get out.
Somehow, I made it out.
And I came to the spot where I was supposed to meet him.
And he wasn't there.
So I made my way back to that village.
And--
That was-- [INAUDIBLE] was the name.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And basically, I used to hang, hang around in the woods and then sneak in and sleep in his barn at night.
And I would go around and ask for food, some of the farmers.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] One time, I walk into this house.
And I notice something suspicious.
A woman like-- when I walked in, this woman ran out.
It turns out that there were Jews being hidden by that family, with a Polish family, [?
Gingalewski. ?] And when they found out who I was, they made a decision that-- one particular woman that they were hiding talked to me.
And she said if I could-- I could give them something, because they're not rich, then maybe I could stay there.
So I had a few things on me.
I had a watch and something.
So I gave them what I had.
And I said, maybe I'll go into my house.
We have some things hidden.
Maybe I can get some more.
They let me stay there over the winter, which was very significant because otherwise, I don't know if I would've made it through that winter.
In the spring, I decided to get out, and find another-- find a job pasturing cows.
So I did.
And I went into this house.
And the husband wasn't there.
But the woman said, oh, yeah, you wait till my husband comes home, because we need somebody.
And then finally-- she gave me food.
And then finally, the husband shows up.
And he-- he spotted me in the woods already.
He knew who I was.
So he says, ah, that's that little Jew.
So it became a problem.
They couldn't take me.
But he wanted me because they needed somebody to pasture their cows.
So I was about ready to leave.
He said, no, wait.
Let me-- I'll tell you, he says, what we're going to do.
My father's coming to visit next week.
So when my father comes, you're going to go back with him to the other village.
And then a week later, he's going to come back and bring you back here.
So this way, it'll be as though his father found the boy pasturing the cows and brought him.
So that's exactly what we did.
You know, I came back when his father came.
And he took me there.
And a week later, he brought me back.
And I stayed there till the summer of '43.
In the summer of '43, the Ukrainian population in that area, who were the majority, decided that the Polish Catholics in that area, who were a small minority, who needs them.
If we get rid of them, we can get their farms and stuff.
So they started-- organized attacks on the Polish villages or colonies.
And it was getting pretty bad.
And somehow, the family that I lived with got word that they'll probably attack this particular area.
So they decided to-- to run.
So they packed up the wagon with all their-- whatever they could take.
And they ran to my town, to Maciejow, for German protection, to the Germans.
And now, I had a choice.
Either I go with them or I stay here and get killed.
So I went with them.
And then when we arrived in the town, the German Gestapo were there and were standing near the church property.
And they were directing everybody in there to stay there for the time being.
One day, one of the girls that came from our village had a relative in town or a friend.
Comes back with this little boy.
And when I saw the little boy, I saw trouble.
The little boy looks at me, looks at me.
And he says to her, you know, here in Maciejow, we had a little Jew that looked exactly like him.
And he happened to be also a neighbor of my grandfather's.
And I had once sort of a problem with him.
He used to climb over to-- to steal, steal raspberries.
And I caught him.
And so I'm sure he didn't like me.
And the girl tried to dissuade him, saying, what are you.
You're comparing one of our Polish Catholics to a Jew?
And the kid got confused.
But he went back and told his parents, his mother, especially.
The next day, when we had to go out and stand in line to get food and to register, I see the mother and the sister standing there.
So I saw it was a problem.
And at that point, my-- the farmer that I lived with, who knew I was Jewish, registered me as Stanislav Kwiatkowski.
He gave me the name.
And I thought that it's not good, that I would have to get away.
And then, a little bit later, that same boy that was chasing me in town, that Olek, shows up.
And he probably was told by the others.
And he spots me and comes over.
And he says to me, what's your name.
And I say Stasiek.
No, no, no, he says.
But what's your real name?
And I sort of played the game that I don't know what he was talking about.
But he knew.
He said, where, where are your Ginsburgs.
He knew.
He wasn't sure, maybe, what to do yet because now the Polish Catholics were running from the Ukrainians, too.
So maybe he didn't know, you know, what decision to make.
Then later on, he comes with a friend to show the miracle to a friend, that's there's a Jew here.
And I saw them coming into the-- to the-- to the room.
And there was a table, a big table, standing there with Polish Catholics registering to go to the other side of Poland.
And they wore these big skirts.
So I made believe something dropped.
And I went under the table.
And they came into the room and looked and looked.
They couldn't-- couldn't spot me.
And they left.
And the other boy was making fun of him.
He says, you're imagining.
There's nobody here.
Just from-- in the spring, I got the job, till the summer.
So it was like--
So now, I had to get out of there.
So I told them-- now, the procedure was that they're going to the railroad station and taking a train over to Chelm, which is Poland, the other side of Poland.
So I told my-- that man that I'm going to go.
I'm going to leave today.
And then he was thinking it over, thinking it over.
And they decided to leave the same time.
So it made it easier for me.
And we made it to the railroad station.
I told him-- of course, there were Germans there controlling.
And I told him that as far as he's concerned, I'm by myself.
If I get captured, then I don't know him, you know.
And then we came to Chelm.
And we were in this house where they were bringing in these refugees, these Polish Catholic refugees that were being killed by the Ukrainians.
And this man came to look for-- needed a boy to pasture cows.
So I guess this man where I lived-- his name was Jan Huber-- probably helped getting me hooked up with him.
And this man came over.
And he told them that my parents were killed by the Ukrainians, that I was a Polish Catholic, of course.
And so he decided to take me.
So from, you know--
So I went.
He took me home.
Being that he was mayor of that area, he went to City Hall.
He made me a document saying that I'm Stanislav Kwiatkowski, with a German swastika on it.
So it was a good document to have, except I didn't need it at that point because I lived with them.
Then at one point, the German SS division moved into that area.
And they took over half of the house of-- where I lived.
So I was a little bit apprehensive.
But, you know, I tried to play like I'm not worried.
So-- And there were two German brothers there.
And when they used to get rations-- they saw me working hard, you know-- they used to give me-- they had candy like Lifesavers.
They used to give me the candy.
And they were-- And then they asked me to polish-- there was a high-ranking officer in that-- they made it like a headquarters.
Yes, they took half of the house.

They used to come in-- like, a military truck used to come in with a kitchen, with-- you know, bringing the meals, because they had-- they had a whole division stationed there that they were feeding in different places.
At one point, you know, they had this motorcycle with-- with an extra thing, you know.
Yeah.
So at one point, they called me.
Stanislav, come!
And I never, you know, when they said come, I didn't say no.
They put me in there.
And the guy was an SS guy with a helmet and bayonet, driving into Chelm.
And I'm sitting on the-- you know, that extra seat, which was not smart because there were a lot of Polish Catholics there from my area.
And I could have been-- I could have been recognized, except it would have blown their mind to say, what is he doing with the SS, you know.

I found out now when I came back that they were worried about me being close to the-- with the Germans, because their house was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was an underground cell of the Polish Underground.
So they, they were a little bit-- they were suspicious of me.
They thought maybe the Germans are using me to get information from them.
But I didn't know.
I didn't know that there was an underground cell there.
So I worked there for about a year.
And then I slept in the same room with this one boy, whom I met recently in '93 when I was there.
And I always was careful.
I watched myself.
And somehow, one night, he spotted that I was circumcised.
And he probably was confused because I used to go to confession.
I went to church.
And so he knew I was Catholic.
So it was confusing to him.
He liked to drink.
So maybe one evening when he had some drinks, he may have told it to others as a fun thing that look, there's a Catholic here that's circumcised.
Imagine.
You know, something.
I don't know how it happened.
But the boys that I was pasturing the cows with were beginning to talk.
And one of them, Riszek, was friendly with me.
He used to, you know, take me on his bike and teach me how to ride a bike.
He came to me and said, Stasiek, I don't believe it.
But they're talking.
And it may be dangerous.
So that's when I decided to leave.
And when I told them I was leaving, they were, like, shocked in a way, because they didn't know what happened.
And I told them that boys are talking and that-- And they actually-- the father wanted me to stay.
But the younger brother, who was in the Underground, said to the father, let him make his on decision, you know.
So then where did you go?
So I went back to that other guy that brought me there.
And I told him-- I told him I don't-- I can't stay there.
They're-- you know-- it's not-- I didn't tell him that they know.
So he told me where to go, where his sister-in-law lived, and find a job there.
So I went there.
I found a job.
Somebody needed to pasture cows.
And I was there only a few days.
And I'm out in the field with the cows.
And suddenly, that-- that guy that sent me there comes with another man on the wagon.
And they call me.
I was a little bit concerned why they were coming there.
I didn't know who it was.
And then I recognized him.
And he, he says, come on.
You're coming with us.
I said, no, I am working here.
Come on!
They got me on the wagon.
They took me away.
That farmer up till today, if he's still alive, never knew what happened to me.
I just disappeared, left the cows.
And I felt bad about it because he treated me nice.
So I came to another farmer, who probably paid this guy for getting me.
Later on, I find out-- I find out from a nephew of his that the uncle captured a Jew hiding in one of the stacks of hay and, you know, pulled him out and took away his boots.
And he gave him over to the Gestapo.
At this whole time when you were-- when you were living as a Catholic, did you feel yourself to be a Catholic?
No, no, I knew who I was.
I, I-- see, I was old enough.
And I had a religious education in Judaism.
So I knew who I was.
I was sort of confused.
I used to have arguments.

Every night, when, when when the son would go down on his knees to say his prayers, I would automatically go down and say the, like the first sentence of the Polish prayer, or the Catholic prayer aloud so he could hear me.
And then I would have an argument with my God, basically saying, what are you doing.
You, you-- what is this?
You know.
It was a confusing situation.
So then you're at this next farm.
So I was in that new place for about three months.
That was already '44.
That was summer-- you know, spring, summer, '44.
And in August of '44, the Russians were closing in, coming.
You know, German-- basically, the German front was falling apart.
But they were still fighting.
So Chelm was already liberated by the Russians.
But some fanatic German division decided to make a stand in the village where I lived, in [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and the Russians saw what was happening.
So they let-- they bypassed it.
They left it alone.
And they kept going.
And then they surrounded the village.
And you were liberated then.
And about a week later, they attacked like full attack.
The roofs were flying.
And they liberated, you know, the village.
And that was that.
I still have arguments.
No, I don't have arguments anymore.
Now, people that get involved in that, I tell them that it's not really God.
They have a problem.
They have a problem in interpretation.
They have a problem in expectation.
What do you expect to God?
God never told anybody that if he's sitting there ready to be shot that he's going to grab the rifle and protect him.

That's not-- it's-- For me, it was very sad to see the most righteous of people in Judaism, old men with white beards that were sincere and religious being taken, you know, by these hoodlums to be shot.
And basically, they saying to God, God, you know, certain prayers, [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
Or why did you forsake me and, and Shema Yisrael.
I felt-- I mean-- Even after the war, I felt very bad that they went to such a death.
But maybe that was our problem.
Maybe the Jews in that part of the world lived a fantasy life where they were so happy in their religion.
On [NON-ENGLISH], we came to the synagogue.
And it was dancing.
And every Saturday night, you know, praying and dancing.
It was a, a, a happy religious life.
And perhaps-- And being, like, concentrated in-- together in towns like a ghetto almost maybe, maybe felt secure.
But obviously, there was no security there.
And younger people that wanted to leave for Palestine, for Israel, at that time were-- were not allowed in a lot of cases by their parents.

A lot of people, especially the younger generation, the Zionists, you know, could have-- a lot of people could have gotten out, although that wasn't easy because the British didn't let you in there, you know, so it was a-- But I have my own thinking and philosophy about religion that people that want to blame God-- don't blame God, because God didn't do it.

Like some religious fanatics, idiots, will come out and say that actually God did this to those Jews over there because they weren't good enough, because the German Jews started assimilating, so therefore, all of them had to pay the price.
Well, I made some-- yeah.
Some-- one of my aunts comes to Chelm and finds out through that man that brought me there and that gardener that I went for food lived in Chelm.
They also had to run away.
So my aunt met that woman gardener.
And-- and she knew from the other guy that I was in the area.
They didn't know where.
Only that guy knew.
So they-- you know, she-- they introduced her.
And she and that man came to the farm to take me, you know, back.
And so I told my farmer that I'm leaving.
And I never told him I was Jewish.
And he was crying.
She says-- he says he liked me because I was a good workhorse.
He said good is not for long, you know.
And I left.
And then we find out that another aunt, who now lives in Connecticut, is in Russia and cannot come back because she doesn't have any papers.
So I decide to make a trip.
And I find out what the story was.
And I go back.
And I get her papers.
And then I end up staying there for less than a year, till, till the war ended, like in May--
Roughly May of '45.
Then I came back, back to, to Poland.
By myself.
But my aunt left earlier.
And then I met her there.
And we went to Germany.
We ended up in the Munich area.
And there, I went to one of the ORT schools, learning, like, trades.
And then I was able to come to the United States with a group of orphans that it was sponsored-- it was like United-- I don't know if it was a combination, United States and United Nations.
It was some group that was sponsoring orphans.
So I came with that group.
And then my grandfather's sister lived here.
So I ended up living with her.
And I went to-- still managed to go to junior high school.
And then I went to Brooklyn Technical High School.
Yeah.
I remember looking out.
We arrived at night.
And looking out of the boat.
And I saw the, the-- all these lights, the skylights lit up.
So I figured they turned on all the lights for us, you know, to greet us.
[LAUGHS] Yeah, so that was an interesting-- yeah.
And I got my pants straight.
And I put them under my mattress to make sure that they're straight.
And we arrived.
